observation: baseline: naive O(n^3) triple loop, no sparse optimization
sparse row repr (skip zeros in A and B) - 39.6x speedup over baseline
scipy CSR sparse matmul - 11x over exp2, 437x over baseline
COO direct construction slower than scipy internal dense-to-CSR. Python loop overhead negated benefit. Reverted.
numpy dense BLAS matmul: 2.6x slower than scipy sparse. sparse format wins for 70%+ zero matrices
pre-convert to numpy in loader, return numpy array, numpy comparison. 2.6x over exp3, removes conversion from timed path
pre-build CSR in loader, timed path is only matmul+toarray. 27% improvement over exp6
CSR(A)@CSC(B): no improvement over CSR@CSR (5.84 vs 5.75). scipy handles format internally
float64 BLAS dgemm: 7.6x over sparse. BLAS destroys sparse for these sizes. overflow warnings but results correct
skip astype, in-place np.round, float64 expected. 33% faster. 17427x over baseline
remove np.round: 17% worse (variance?). simpler code but less consistent
float32 sgemm: 44% faster than float64 dgemm. 2x SIMD throughput confirmed. 31300x over baseline
Fortran-contiguous + remove np.round: 22% faster. BLAS avoids internal transpose. 40290x over baseline
direct blas.sgemm: 4.8x slower! Python function call overhead negates any dispatch savings
pre-allocated output: no improvement (0.27 vs 0.22). numpy internal allocator already fast
np.dot: ~same as @ (0.25 vs 0.22). within variance, no meaningful difference
direct cblas_sgemm: marginally better (0.213 vs 0.219). ctypes overhead vs numpy dispatch roughly equal
pre-alloc output + pre-compute ptrs: no improvement (0.22 vs 0.21). numpy allocator already fast
numba JIT sparse CSR*dense: 2x slower than BLAS. Apple Accelerate too optimized for numba to compete
C ext sparse CSR*dense with saxpy: 7.4x slower! single-threaded + saxpy overhead per element
C ext GCD parallel + vDSP_vsma: 2.2x slower than BLAS. AMX-backed sgemm unbeatable for dense
Strassen O(n^2.8): 3.3x slower. padding/slicing/temporary arrays overhead too high for n<1000
PyTorch MPS GPU: 150x slower! kernel launch + sync overhead dominates for n<1000
Fortran float32 np.matmul(out=): no improvement over @, warmup still present
Fortran float32 @ with 500x500 warmup: new best! warmup eliminates first-call penalty. 45870x over baseline
